insight: When two independent advisor lenses (e.g., Simplicity + Organizational) converge on the same simplification, treat it as strong signal and take it. In this session, both flagged R22 per-file persist-back routing as over-engineered for v1; collapsing it to a single 'save whole tree to active profile' choice cut estimated dev time ~40% with no loss of day-one user value. Same-direction signal across different lenses is much stronger than within-lens elaboration.

Atomic directory replacement uses the pending/prior rename pattern: (a) populate .pending/, (b) rename existing canonical to .prior/, (c) rename .pending/ to canonical. Recovery is symmetric — if .prior/ exists at startup, rename it back; if only .pending/ exists, discard. This is mechanically simpler than 'try operation, catch errors, rollback' logic because rename() is the only atomic primitive POSIX gives you for directories. Pair with a lockfile (PID + timestamp, kill -0 staleness check) for concurrency, and use temp-file + rename for the state file so readers never see torn writes. These three primitives (pending/prior, lockfile, atomic state write) together cover the bulk of crash-recovery requirements for FS-mutating CLIs.

Always include schemaVersion in any state/config file the tool writes and reads, from day one. Treat unparseable or schema-mismatched files as a benign degraded state (e.g., NoActive), never a fatal error. Cost is one field; benefit is forward-compatibility forever. Cross-checked by both interface designer and STPA control structure analyst in this session as a non-negotiable. The pattern: { schemaVersion: 1, ...rest }; readers validate; on mismatch, log a warning and behave as if the file didn't exist.

ResolvedPlan schema lock pattern: when an epic mandates a contract freeze (per fitness function), build the contract doc co-located with code (docs/specs/contracts/<contract>.md) listing schemaVersion, invariants, error-trigger rules, and worked examples. Cite the EARS subset that constrains each field and ensure tests assert each invariant. Add forward-compat clauses up front (additive optional fields don't bump version).

When implementing per-type merge with strategy pattern: keep the strategy function signature byte-pure ((relPath, ContributorBytes[]) => StrategyResult). Move all IO into a thin orchestrator with an injectable read() so unit tests of strategies are sync, fast, and disk-free, while integration tests round-trip through the real fs via the resolver.

JSON.parse produces __proto__ as own enumerable property; deep-merge over a JSON object must use Object.defineProperty (not direct assignment) to preserve __proto__ as a literal data key — otherwise the prototype setter elides it from JSON.stringify output, causing silent data loss

When implementing pending/prior atomic-rename protocol on Node.js, use per-attempt unique tmp file paths (e.g. .lock.<pid>.<nonce>.tmp) rather than a shared canonical .lock.tmp. Two concurrent acquirers racing on a shared tmp path can produce a verify-read fall-through where the loser's verify sees a stale PID and silently proceeds. Pair with a bounded retry loop (3 attempts) when verify-read shows a dead-PID lock — re-claim by retrying acquire from scratch, only escalate to LockHeldError on a live racer. This is the same lockfile pattern used by git.

On Windows, process.kill(pid, 0) is an unreliable liveness check for cross-process locks: PID recycling is fast and cross-session/cross-user PIDs return ambiguous results (true for unknown reasons). Supplement kill(0) with a max-age threshold (e.g. 1 hour) on win32 only — if the lock timestamp is older than the threshold, treat as stale even when kill(0) returns success. POSIX kill(0) semantics are unambiguous (ESRCH = definitely dead) so this guard is platform-conditional.

Two-tier fingerprint comparison must split metadata walk from content hashing. Walk live tree once for stat() (size+mtime) only; for files whose metadata diverges from the recorded fingerprint, lazily read+hash that single file. Eagerly hashing the entire tree in fingerprintTree() then short-circuiting in compareFingerprint() does the IO anyway and negates the fast-path's purpose. The savings matter at 1000+ files where hashing dominates the drift-check latency budget.

Pure decision functions are the right granularity for state machines whose IO half is owned by another epic. E4's gate decision (decideGate) is a pure function over (DriftReport, mode, --on-drift= flag) returning {kind: 'no-drift'|'auto'|'prompt', choice}. The interactive prompt itself lives in E5. This split lets E4 own the invariant tests (hard-block, no infinite block in non-interactive) while E5 owns IO. Same pattern applies wherever a 'must prompt the user' decision can be cleanly separated from the prompt itself.

Fail-open invariants in shell-hook entry points must wrap stderr writes in try/catch. process.stderr.write can throw EPIPE synchronously when the parent (e.g. git pre-commit driver) closes the pipe. If the throw escapes, the function rejects and process.exit(0) never runs — the hook ends up with a non-zero exit despite the entire body being designed to never block. Pattern: outer try/catch around the whole side-effect block + inner try/catch per write that breaks out cleanly on first failure. Lose the message, never block the commit.

When a degraded-state warning code differentiates 'normal absence' from 'actually broken', surface only the broken cases in user-facing entry points like pre-commit hooks. E4's StateReadWarning has Missing|ParseError|SchemaMismatch; Missing is the normal fresh-project case (no init has run yet) and should NOT emit a warning on every commit. ParseError/SchemaMismatch indicate genuine corruption and SHOULD warn. Suppressing Missing keeps the hook invisible in the happy path while still surfacing the cases users need to know about.

Use default import (`import process from 'node:process'`) NOT namespace import (`import * as process from 'node:process'`). The namespace form exposes internals like `_events` and `_eventsCount` but loses EventEmitter prototype methods (`process.on`) and TTY props (`process.stdin.isTTY`). Tests with signal handlers disabled work fine because they never call `.on`; production fails at runtime when bin tries to register SIGINT handler with 'process.on is not a function', AND TTY detection silently degrades to `undefined && undefined === false` so non-interactive code paths trigger in real terminals. Reproduce: `node -e "import('node:process').then(p => console.log(typeof p.on))"` prints 'undefined'.

When testing modules that register process signal handlers (e.g. lock cleanup), make the signalHandlers opt REQUIRED at every layer of the call chain — never optional with a default-true. The default-true form lets a test forget to pass false, causing a vitest worker error like 'Cannot redefine property: _eventsCount' at handler-registration time, which is confusing because it points at the lock module, not the test. Required-boolean forces every caller (bin always true, tests always false) to be explicit. The slight extra typing is worth it: omitting the param becomes a type error caught at test-write time, not a runtime crash inside framework code.

Pattern for end-to-end SIGINT/SIGTERM cleanup tests: write a tiny ESM helper script (.mjs, no compilation step) that invokes the production code path (e.g. `await withLock(paths, async () => { process.stdout.write('READY\n'); await heartbeat(); })`), then have the test spawn it, wait for the 'READY' marker on stdout, send the signal, and assert (a) exit code === 128 + signo (SIGINT=130, SIGTERM=143) and (b) the on-disk artifact is gone. Critical detail: `await new Promise(() => {})` exits immediately on Node 20+ with 'Warning: Detected unsettled top-level await'. Pair the unsettled promise with a long-period `setInterval(() => {}, 60_000)` to keep the event loop alive so the signal can fire. Without the heartbeat, Node bails before any signal can be delivered.

Enforce CLI --json mode invariants once in the OutputChannel, not in every command. Pattern: createOutput({json}) returns {print, json, warn, error}; print() and warn() are NO-OPs in json mode while error() always writes and json() always emits. Command code branches on `output.jsonMode` to choose payload SHAPE (human strings vs structured payload) but never branches on whether to write — the channel is a hard guard. This collapses the 'silence human output' epic invariant into one place that's impossible to bypass from a command file. Counter-pattern (rejected): each command checking `if (jsonMode) { json(...) } else { print(...) }` for every line — leaks a stray print() and you've broken the contract.

CLI exit-code policy: centralise the error→code mapping in one exitCodeFor(err) function and one CliUserError class that carries its own exitCode. Command handlers never call process.exit and never invent their own exit-code arithmetic — they throw, and a single catch in bin.ts maps to a code via exitCodeFor. Concrete map for a CLI sitting on a typed error hierarchy: ResolverError subclasses + LockHeldError → 3 (conflict/missing class), MergeError → 2 (runtime IO drift), CliNotImplementedError → 2 (system-class stub for cross-epic deferral), unknown → 2, CliUserError → its own (typically 1). The matrix becomes a small unit-test table that locks the contract; any new error class is one switch arm to register.

Non-TTY hard-block UX pattern (paired with E4's pure decideGate lesson L29affb99): when a CLI session is non-interactive AND a decision flag was not pre-supplied, exit 1 IMMEDIATELY with a message that names the flag the user should add (e.g. 'pass --on-drift=discard|persist|abort'). Never block waiting on stdin — CI scripts hang forever. Never auto-pick a default — silently destructive. The pattern is: detect non-TTY in the bin entry (`process.stdin.isTTY && process.stdout.isTTY`), pass mode='non-interactive' down, and have the orchestrator throw CliUserError with the literal flag name in the message. Test by spawning the bin against a piped stdin () and asserting exit code AND the message contains the flag string.

Cross-epic imports must always go through the epic's barrel (./<epic>/index.js), even when the importing module lives in the same monorepo. Direct submodule imports (../resolver/paths.js → buildPaths) silently couple the consumer to the producer's internal layout — a refactor inside the producer epic breaks consumers without any type signal. If a symbol the consumer needs isn't on the barrel, ADD it (and document why on the public surface) rather than reach in. Concrete check: grep imports for direct paths into peer-epic submodules; route through barrels and add the missing exports. This was a P1 architecture finding for E5 because two CLI commands imported buildPaths and isValidProfileName directly from src/resolver/paths.js.

Node 20 readline.promises.question() does NOT reject when the input stream closes (Ctrl-D / pipe end) — it just hangs. To enforce an EOF contract, wire an AbortController to rl.once('close', () => ac.abort()) and pass {signal} into question(). The signal-aware path rejects with AbortError on close; without it, your test will time out and your CLI prompt will hang.

When a verb in a multi-verb CLI has a 'fail-open / soft-degrade' edge case (e.g. hook install in a non-git project) AND a 'fail-loud / surface error' edge case, expose both via a single Boolean opt like `allowSkip`. The two callers (init flow vs explicit `hook install` verb) get the right semantics by passing different values, and the underlying primitive stays a single function with one error-path. Pattern observed in claude-code-profiles E6: init passes allowSkip=true so a non-git bootstrap doesn't abort, while `hook install` passes false so the user sees the error.
